characters.physical_details: Lean, wiry build — the body of someone who walks a lot and eats inconsistently. Rough hands from years of manual labor and ritual work. Sun-weathered skin, dark hair usually unkempt. Plain-featured, not distinctive-looking, which has helped him blend in for years. His eyes look normal — System-blindness is an internal condition, not a visible one. No scars or distinguishing marks at story start.

key_relationships.aldric: unwilling host — the paladin's consciousness inhabits his mind from Chapter 1
key_relationships.vess: unwilling host — the assassin's fragment enters in Chapter 4, leaves in Chapter 16
key_relationships.derry: oldest friend, fraud partner for eleven years — betrays Maren in Chapter 13
key_relationships.thessa: antagonist-turned-grudging-mentor — the real sin-eater who despises his fraud
key_relationships.caul: pursuer — the hero-ranger tracking his bounty

characters.occupation_status: Fraudulent sin-eater for eleven years (ages 17-28); transitions to genuine sin-eater over the course of the novel
aliases: the sin-eater (how Fenhold villagers know him)
characters.literacy_languages: Literate in the common language. Can read and write — his fraud required reading people's reactions to System prompts and mimicking appropriate responses, which demands intelligence if not education.

state_transitions.change: Receives Aldric's consciousness, combat training, and memories; loses the ability to maintain his fraud
System-registered for the first time; bounty placed; loses anonymity
Receives Vess's fragment; the collision creates an unclassified entity within him
Kills for the first time; acquires the physical and psychological consequences of violence
First partially successful genuine sin-eating consumption; physical toll
Loses Vess through separation; diminished tactical ability, lonelier
Performs consumption on the System's wound; sees the interface for the first and only time; chooses to become a real sin-eater

characters.availability: Present in every chapter. The novel is locked to his perspective.

characters.physical_details: Dead. Was approximately 35 when he died — tall, broad, fair-haired, the build of a man trained for combat and maintained by duty. Classic paladin appearance. Existed as a fragment of consciousness inside Maren's mind, with no physical form. Maren has Aldric's memories of his body but not his body.

key_relationships.maren: reluctant host — Aldric inhabits Maren's consciousness; despises his morals but chose him deliberately
key_relationships.vess: antagonist within the skull — opposed classification (holy/order vs. shadow/guild); constant friction until her departure
key_relationships.order_of_the_veil: former member — a paladin order that investigated System anomalies; may have gotten him killed

characters.occupation_status: Was a paladin of the Order of the Veil for fourteen years. Died in a dungeon collapse that may have been a System correction. Now exists as a consciousness fragment.
aliases: the paladin (how Maren usually thinks of him)
characters.literacy_languages: Highly literate. Trained in System theory, religious texts, and military documentation. Can read the System interface and describe it to Maren.

state_transitions.change: Enters Maren's consciousness; begins reading the interface
Sees something in the fragment collision that silences him
Reveals what he has been seeing; admits he chose Maren deliberately
Vess's departure leaves him as the sole fragment; his dynamic with Maren shifts
Settles into a changed relationship with Maren — less sarcastic, more honest, but still present and still himself

characters.availability: Present from Chapter 1 through the end. Internal voice only — no physical presence.

characters.physical_details: Dead. Was approximately 30 when she died — small, dark-haired, sharp-featured. Built for unobtrusiveness, not attention. Nightguild training emphasized physical ordinariness. Exists as a consciousness fragment inside Maren, with gaps — incomplete memories, truncated emotions.

key_relationships.maren: reluctant host — she did not choose to be consumed and resents it
key_relationships.aldric: antagonist within the skull — ideological and classificatory opposition; argued constantly
key_relationships.torren_blackhall: unfinished contract target — a noble who used quest access to cover a massacre

characters.occupation_status: Was a Nightguild assassin. Mid-contract when consumed by a quest she was exploiting for access to her target. Incomplete fragment — has memory gaps and emotional truncation.
aliases: the assassin (how Maren initially thinks of her)
characters.literacy_languages: Literate in common language and Nightguild operational codes. Can assess tactical situations and read System data related to threat analysis.

state_transitions.change: Enters Maren's consciousness through botched consumption; immediately hostile
History revealed — the contract, Blackhall, her incompleteness, the missing sister's name
Shows unexpected gentleness during Maren's recovery from consumption attempt
Chooses to separate from Maren through reversed sin-eating; dissipates as an unhosted fragment; releases the Blackhall contract

characters.availability: Present from Chapter 4 through Chapter 16. Internal voice only. After Chapter 16, gone.

characters.physical_details: Stocky, farmer's build. Red-brown hair, open face. Looks trustworthy because he mostly is. Hands that know soil and livestock. Sweats visibly when nervous, which is often after Chapter 3.

key_relationships.maren: oldest friend, fraud partner for eleven years — chooses his daughter over Maren in Chapter 13
key_relationships.lenna: wife — waiting in Fenhold with their daughter
key_relationships.pell: daughter — young, learning to count; the reason Derry ultimately leaves

characters.occupation_status: Farmer in Fenhold. Supplemented income by helping Maren fake sin-eating rituals — feeding him System details Maren couldn't see.
characters.literacy_languages: Functionally literate. Can read System prompts and basic documents but not a reader by habit.

state_transitions.change: Leaves Fenhold with Maren; separates from wife and daughter
Trust in Maren visibly erodes after Caul's moral confrontation
Makes contact with Caul; tells him their direction; leaves Maren and heads home

characters.availability: Present Chapters 1-13. Leaves in Chapter 13. Not present in Chapters 14-18.

characters.physical_details: Tall, angular, grey-streaked hair kept short for practicality. Hands permanently stained from decades of ritual work — a visible record of contamination exposure. Lean from physical labor, not diet. Moves with the efficiency of someone who has been walking contaminated terrain for thirty years. Looks tired in a permanent way.

key_relationships.maren: antagonist-turned-grudging-colleague — furious about his fraud, eventually acknowledges his capability

characters.occupation_status: Professional sin-eater for approximately thirty years. The only genuine sin-eater working her region. Based in Silt but travels extensively for contamination clearing.
characters.literacy_languages: Fully literate. Expert knowledge of sin-eating ritual texts, contamination theory, and System maintenance protocols.

state_transitions.change: Introduced; confronts Maren about his fraud; refuses to help
Present at the Crook; argues for consuming Maren; then witnesses his wider-area capability and begins teaching him
Acknowledges Maren as a colleague — gives him his next assignment

characters.availability: Present in Chapters 7, 14, 15, 18. Referenced in other chapters.

characters.physical_details: Weathered, military bearing. Ranger gear — practical, field-worn, not ceremonial. Favors his right knee slightly from an old injury. Has the look of someone who used to smile more. Clean-shaven, hair kept short, the grooming of a man who maintains routine because routine is all he has.

key_relationships.maren: pursuer — tracks Maren's bounty; the moral antagonist of the novel
key_relationships.haley: dead party member — one of two friends killed by quest contamination three months before the novel begins
key_relationships.renn: dead party member — the other friend killed in the same incident

characters.occupation_status: Hero-ranger. Active duty. Has been tracking fake sin-eaters since losing his party to uncleared contamination.
characters.literacy_languages: Fully literate. Trained in System navigation, field reporting, threat assessment.

state_transitions.change: Confronts Maren; delivers moral accusation; gives a day's head start toward the Crook
Arrives at the Crook with a full party; witnesses the System's direct response; stops fighting Maren to fight the System's correction; his faith in the System fractures

characters.availability: Present in Chapters 10, 15. Referenced (as a pursuing threat) in Chapters 3, 9, 13.

timeline.story_start: Late summer — the harvest season, when sin-eating demand is highest because farmers die in the fields and contamination must be cleared before it spoils the crop
timeline.estimated_span: Approximately four weeks from the inciting ritual to the final consumption at the Woundmouth

seasonal_track.season: Late summer — hot, dusty roads, oppressive warmth, quest-light shimmering in heat haze
Summer turning to early autumn — first cooler nights, longer shadows, harvest ending
Early autumn — cooler air, changing light, the sense of a season closing

key_events.event: Maren's ritual over the dead farmer; Aldric's arrival
System bounty placed on Maren; flight from Fenhold
Botched consumption; Vess's arrival; unclassified entity born
First killing of a bounty hunter
Aldric's revelation about the System's architecture
Arrival at the Crook; sin-eater examination
Caul's attack on the Crook; System's direct response
Maren's first real sin-eating as a genuine practitioner

geography.primary_setting: A secondary-world fantasy landscape organized by the System — a pervasive interface that classifies all entities, assigns quests, and generates the infrastructure of heroism. The terrain is rural, agricultural, punctuated by dungeon mouths and contamination zones.

key_locations.name: Fenhold
key_locations.details: Maren's home village. Rural, agricultural, on the edge of dungeon territory. Poor but functioning. Maren has been the village sin-eater (fraudulently) for eleven years. The village depends on sin-eating to keep farmland clear of quest contamination. A day's walk from the nearest dungeon mouth.

key_locations.name: Silt
key_locations.details: A market town half a day off the main road. Larger than Fenhold — a few hundred people, a market square, an inn. Where Thessa works and lives. Known for practical trades, not heroism.

key_locations.name: Harrow
key_locations.details: A hero staging town near active dungeon mouths. Several days' travel past Silt. Built around the hero economy: quest boards, gear shops, taverns with damage rankings displayed overhead, scorch marks on every road from hero-class abilities. Heavily System-saturated — the interface is denser here than anywhere else in the story.

key_locations.name: The Crook
key_locations.details: A seasonal sin-eater gathering place in a valley between three dungeon mouths. Not a permanent settlement — a campsite that sin-eaters use during heavy contamination season. Located in high-contamination territory, which is why sin-eaters gather there: the work is densest. Several days past Harrow, deeper into dungeon territory.

key_locations.name: The Woundmouth
key_locations.details: A specific dungeon mouth where the System's architecture is thinnest. Deep in dungeon territory, past the Crook. Quest-light does not pool here but bleeds — a continuous seepage rather than collected pools. The contamination is so old it has become structural, part of the landscape. The air tastes different. The hum is louder. This is where the System's containment is most strained.

distances: Fenhold to Silt: approximately 5-6 days on foot, following the main road
Silt to Harrow: approximately 3-4 days on foot, roads increasingly hero-patrolled
Harrow to the Crook: approximately 4-5 days on foot, moving deeper into dungeon territory where roads deteriorate
The Crook to the Woundmouth: approximately 3 days on foot through heavy contamination territory
Overall journey Fenhold to Woundmouth: approximately 3-4 weeks of travel with stops

world_rules: The System is a pervasive interface visible to all humans from birth, except the extremely rare System-blind (estimated 1 in 100,000). It assigns classes, levels, skills, and quests. It generates bounties, threat assessments, and navigation data.

System-blindness is not a visible condition — a System-blind person's eyes look normal. It is an internal inability to perceive or interact with the System interface. The System cannot write data onto a System-blind person's consciousness.

Quest-light is a physical phenomenon — a pale, slightly warm luminescence that pools like water outside dungeon mouths and in areas of quest residue. It is visible to everyone, not just the System-sighted, though System-sighted people see it with overlaid data (quest details, threat levels) while the System-blind see only the raw glow.
Scorch marks are residual System energy left by hero-class abilities on roads, walls, and stone surfaces. They are permanent and no one maintains or cleans them — they accumulate like graffiti.
Dungeon mouths are physical openings in hillsides, cliff faces, or the ground, marked by concentrations of quest-light. They generate quests and spawn threat creatures.

Sin-eaters are a recognized System class/role. They consume completed or abandoned quests to clear residual contamination from areas. The work is unglamorous, essential, and low-status — spiritual janitors maintaining the infrastructure others take for granted.

Proper sin-eating requires a specific ritual sequence and genuine openness to contamination. The ritual dissolves both the contamination and any fragments (residual consciousnesses) embedded in it. Fraudulent sin-eating — performing the ritual without genuine engagement — leaves contamination and fragments intact.

Fragments are the residual consciousnesses of dead heroes, adventurers, or other individuals whose deaths occurred in areas of quest contamination. They are embedded in the contamination and gain coherence over time if not consumed by a proper sin-eater. A fragment retains partial memories, skills, personality, and sensory capabilities from the original person.

When fragments are consumed by a System-blind host, the dissolution process does not activate (the System cannot write the dissolution onto a System-blind consciousness). Instead, the fragments embed as persistent, communicating presences — voices in the host's mind with their own wills, memories, and partial capabilities.

The System classifies everything. An unclassified entity triggers automatic System responses — escalating from bounties (incentivizing heroes to eliminate the unknown) to direct structural corrections (reclassifying the area as a threat zone, spawning countermeasures).
System bounties are visible on the interface to all System-sighted heroes. The bounty value reflects the System's threat assessment of the target.

The System is, at its deepest architecture, a containment structure — a wall built to hold something older and wilder in check. Sin-eaters were designed as maintenance crews for this wall. This is not common knowledge; the Order of the Veil investigated it, and investigation may have gotten Aldric killed.

Areas of high System activity have an audible low hum that System-sighted people barely notice but that the System-blind can hear clearly. The hum intensifies near dungeon mouths and contamination zones.

power_structure.mechanism: Classification, quest assignment, bounty generation, resource allocation. The System is infrastructure, not a conscious ruler — it maintains order through automated processes rather than deliberate governance.
Organized hero parties operate under guild structures. Guilds mediate between the System's quest assignments and individual heroes. They enforce bounty contracts.
Semi-independent operators. The System classifies them and assigns them areas, but the work is largely self-directed. They are necessary but low-status — the sanitation workers of the hero economy.
A paladin order that studied the System's deeper architecture. Aldric's order. Their investigation of the System's containment function may have led to the order's suppression or dissolution.

Standard feudal-adjacent authority. They hire sin-eaters, host heroes, and depend on the System for economic stability. Torren Blackhall represents the corrupt end of this class — using quest access for personal purposes.

objects.name: Maren's ceramic eating bowl
objects.origin: Ordinary ceramic bowl purchased from a market in Fenhold when Maren first began faking rituals at age 17. No System properties whatsoever.
objects.status: Carried throughout the novel. Used as prop in fake rituals, then as tool in genuine sin-eating. Used for the separation ritual in Chapter 16.

objects.name: Aldric's veil-sigil
objects.origin: The symbol of the Order of the Veil — worn on Aldric's armor in life. Now exists only as a memory Maren carries from Aldric's consciousness.
objects.status: Referenced in memories. No physical existence.

income_sources.amount: Enough to eat and maintain a small dwelling — approximately 12-15 coppers per ritual, with rituals occurring roughly monthly
income_sources.reliability: Was steady for eleven years. Lost when Maren flees Fenhold in Chapter 3. No income source after that — Maren survives on charity, Derry's supplies, and what he can scavenge.

knowledge_state.knows: He has been faking sin-eating for eleven years. He is System-blind. He now carries Aldric's and later Vess's consciousness.
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Fenhold villagers (fraud — until the bounty makes it obvious); Caul (specific connection between Maren's fraud and contamination deaths)

knowledge_state.knows: Maren is a fraud and has been since the beginning. He helped maintain the fraud by feeding Maren System details.
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Fenhold villagers (before the bounty), Thessa (initially), Caul (initially)

knowledge_state.knows: Maren was faking. The System is a containment structure. His own death may have been a System correction. He chose to reach for Maren specifically.
knowledge_state.learned_in: chapter_01 (Maren's fraud), pre-death investigation (System architecture), chapter_04 onward (observations from inside a System-blind host)
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Maren (withholds what he sees until Chapter 12; withholds that he chose Maren until the same chapter)

knowledge_state.knows: Her contract target Torren Blackhall and the massacre he committed. Her own incompleteness — the gaps in her memory, including her sister's missing name.
knowledge_state.learned_in: Pre-death (contract), post-consumption (awareness of her own gaps)
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Maren (initially — her history emerges in Chapter 8); she does not know about the System's containment nature

knowledge_state.knows: Maren is a fraud (confirmed in Chapter 7). The specific contamination damage caused by eleven years of fake sin-eating. That the real sin-eating is getting harder and contamination is growing denser.
knowledge_state.learned_in: chapter_07 (Maren's fraud confirmed), decades of experience (contamination trends)
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Everyone (her private doubt about whether the methods still work)

knowledge_state.knows: His party died from contamination in a village with a certified sin-eater. He is tracking the high-value bounty on an unclassified entity.
knowledge_state.learned_in: Three months before the novel (party's death), chapter_10 area (bounty tracking)
knowledge_state.hidden_from: He does not know specifically that Maren's fraud caused the contamination that killed his party — the connection is circumstantial and Maren does not confess

environmental_constants: Quest-light: a pale, slightly warm luminescence that pools like water in low places outside dungeon mouths and in areas of quest residue. Visible to everyone. System-sighted people see it overlaid with data; Maren sees only the raw glow — faint shimmer, like moonlight caught in water.

Scorch marks: permanent residual burn marks on roads, stone surfaces, and walls left by hero-class abilities. Darker than normal stone, slightly warm to the touch. No one maintains or cleans them — they accumulate over years, layering older marks under newer ones.

The hum: areas of high System activity produce an audible low-frequency hum. System-sighted people filter it out unconsciously. Maren has always heard it clearly — a constant background presence that intensifies near dungeon mouths and contamination zones. At the Woundmouth, the hum is almost painful.

Contamination smell: quest contamination has a specific odor — ozone-like, slightly sweet, with an undertone of decay. Stronger contamination smells more acrid. Sin-eaters and people who live near dungeon mouths recognize it instinctively. Fresh contamination smells different from old contamination.

Dungeon mouths: physical openings marked by dense quest-light. The air near them is cooler and carries the contamination smell. The ground around them is often discolored — a darker hue from years of quest-light exposure.

Road conditions: main roads between settlements are maintained but show heavy use — ruts, scorch marks, quest-light residue in the ditches. Roads deteriorate as you move deeper into dungeon territory. Past the Crook, roads are barely paths.
